module: Module 9: Slope and Rate of Change

sections.text: Welcome back. Algebra One, Module Nine — slope and rate of change. If you've ever climbed stairs and gotten tired faster on a steeper one, you already feel slope. Today we measure it.

Quick scene. You're walking up two staircases. Same number of steps. The first one is gentle — your knees barely notice. The second one is steep — you're winded by step five. What's the difference? Each step on the steep one rises higher for the same horizontal distance. That ratio — how much you go up versus how much you go across — that is slope. We're just going to put a number on it.

Game plan. First, the formula — rise over run. Second, slope from two points. Third, slope from a graph. Fourth, what positive, negative, zero, and undefined slopes look like. Fifth, slope as a rate of change in real life — speed, growth, cost. By the end you'll be able to look at any line and know exactly how steep it is and what direction it's going.

The formula. Slope equals rise over run. Rise is how much y changes — up or down. Run is how much x changes — left or right. We write it as m equals y two minus y one, all over x two minus x one. Two points, four numbers, one division. That's the whole formula. Memorize it once and you're set for the next four years of math.

Example one. Find the slope between the points one comma two and four comma eight. Subtract the y values — eight minus two equals six. That's our rise. Subtract the x values — four minus one equals three. That's our run. Six over three is two. Slope equals two. What does that mean? For every one step right, the line goes up two. Steep, but climbable.

Example two. Points are negative two comma five and three comma negative five. Y two minus y one — negative five minus five — that's negative ten. X two minus x one — three minus negative two — that's five. Negative ten over five is negative two. Negative slope means the line is going downhill as you read left to right. Same steepness as before, just falling instead of rising.
Your turn. Find the slope between zero comma four and six comma sixteen. Pause the video, do the math, come back. Don't peek.

Time. The answer is two. Sixteen minus four is twelve, six minus zero is six, twelve over six is two. If you got it — perfect. If you flipped y and x by accident — common mistake, do it twice and you'll never do it again.

Now from a graph. You don't always have point coordinates handed to you — sometimes you just have a line. Pick any two points the line clearly passes through. Count squares up — that's your rise. Count squares right — that's your run. Divide. Done. Pro tip: pick points where the line crosses grid intersections cleanly. Saves you from estimating.

Four flavors of slope. Positive slope — line goes uphill, slope is a positive number. Negative slope — line goes downhill, slope is negative. Zero slope — flat, horizontal line, no rise at all. Undefined slope — vertical line, no run at all, and you can't divide by zero, so we don't even give it a number. We just say undefined and move on.

Slope is everywhere in real life. Driving sixty kilometers per hour? That's a slope — sixty kilometers of distance per one hour of time. Saving five hundred dollars a month? That's a slope — five hundred dollars per month. A road sign that says eight percent grade? That's a slope of zero point zero eight. When you hear the phrase rate of change, that's just slope wearing a different jacket.
One more for you. A water tank starts at fifty liters and after six hours has thirty two liters left. Find the rate of change in liters per hour. Pause and try.

Welcome back. Treat the time as x and water as y. Points are zero comma fifty and six comma thirty two. Thirty two minus fifty is negative eighteen. Six minus zero is six. Negative eighteen over six is negative three. The tank is losing three liters per hour. Negative slope makes sense — the water is going down. Beautiful.

One thing to watch out for. The order matters. If you start with point one's y, you have to start with point one's x. Don't mix them. The most common slope mistake on tests is doing y two minus y one on top, but x one minus x two on bottom — that flips the sign on you. Pick your point one, stick with it for both numerator and denominator, and you're safe.

Recap. Slope equals rise over run, that's m equals y two minus y one over x two minus x one. Positive goes up, negative goes down, zero is flat, vertical is undefined. In real life, slope is rate of change — speed, savings, leakage, anything per anything. Always pick your point one and stay consistent. That's it. Your assignment is in the dashboard — ten point pairs to compute, plus one real-world data set to plot. Next up: Module Ten, where we use slope to write actual line equations. See you there.
